items.prompt_md: Why does the classic binary search loop use `while lo <= hi` instead of `while lo < hi`?
options: Because when `lo == hi` there is still exactly one unchecked candidate — `nums[lo]` — that could be the target, so the loop must run once more before giving up
Because using `<` would cause an infinite loop no matter what
Because the array might be empty and `<=` handles that case specially
Because `lo` must always start out strictly less than `hi`
items.answer: Because when `lo == hi` there is still exactly one unchecked candidate — `nums[lo]` — that could be the target, so the loop must run once more before giving up
items.explanation_md: `lo <= hi` keeps the range non-empty. The moment `lo` passes `hi`, every index has been ruled out; but while they're still equal, one element remains to be checked.

items.prompt_md: What does `mid = (lo + hi) // 2` give you, and why is checking `nums[mid]` the right move?
options: The index roughly halfway between `lo` and `hi`; comparing it against the target tells you which half of the remaining range can be discarded entirely
The average value stored in the array, used to estimate where the target might be
A random index, used only to avoid always checking the same spot
The index of the smallest remaining element in `nums[lo..hi]`
items.answer: The index roughly halfway between `lo` and `hi`; comparing it against the target tells you which half of the remaining range can be discarded entirely
items.explanation_md: Because the range is sorted, one comparison against the midpoint is enough to know the target can't be on the wrong side — so that whole half is thrown away.

items.prompt_md: In the branch `if nums[mid] < target: lo = mid + 1`, why does `lo` move to `mid + 1` rather than just `mid`?
options: `nums[mid]` has already been ruled out as too small, so it can never be the answer again and must be excluded from the new range
It doesn't matter — `mid` and `mid + 1` behave identically here
Because `lo` always increases by exactly one per iteration, regardless of `mid`
To make sure the loop runs for at least `log2(n)` iterations
items.answer: `nums[mid]` has already been ruled out as too small, so it can never be the answer again and must be excluded from the new range
items.explanation_md: Once `nums[mid] < target` is known, re-including index `mid` in the next range would be wasted (or worse, could stop the range from shrinking), so the new lower bound skips past it.

items.prompt_md: Why does this technique require the array to be sorted before it will work at all?
options: Sortedness is what makes "is the target too small or too big compared to nums[mid]?" a question with a single, consistent answer across the whole range — without it you can't safely discard a half
It doesn't actually require sortedness; it works on any array
Sorted arrays are required only so `mid` can be computed without overflow
Because `lo` and `hi` must be equal at the start
items.answer: Sortedness is what makes "is the target too small or too big compared to nums[mid]?" a question with a single, consistent answer across the whole range — without it you can't safely discard a half
items.explanation_md: If the array weren't sorted, a comparison at `mid` wouldn't tell you which side the target is on — the target could be anywhere regardless of whether `nums[mid]` is bigger or smaller.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of binary search on a sorted array of n elements?
items.explanation_md: Each comparison discards half of the remaining candidates, so the range shrinks to one element after about log2(n) steps.

items.prompt_md: How does binary search's time complexity compare to a plain linear scan for finding a target in a sorted array of n elements?
options: Binary search is O(log n) versus O(n) for a linear scan
Both are O(n), but binary search has a smaller constant factor
Binary search is O(n) and linear scan is O(log n)
Both are O(log n) once the array is sorted
items.answer: Binary search is O(log n) versus O(n) for a linear scan
items.explanation_md: A linear scan may have to touch every element in the worst case, while binary search halves the remaining candidates on every comparison.

items.prompt_md: What is the loop invariant that makes this binary search correct?
options: If the target is present in the array, it is always somewhere inside `nums[lo..hi]` at the start of every iteration
`nums[mid]` always equals the target by the third iteration
The array is re-sorted after every comparison
items.answer: If the target is present in the array, it is always somewhere inside `nums[lo..hi]` at the start of every iteration
items.explanation_md: Every branch only narrows `lo..hi` to a sub-range that's guaranteed to still contain the target if it exists at all, which is exactly why shrinking the range down to nothing correctly means "not found".

items.prompt_md: A learner rewrites the loop but leaves `lo` and `hi` unchanged in the `nums[mid] < target` branch. What happens?
options: The loop runs forever, re-checking the same `mid` every time, since the search range never shrinks
The function still returns the correct index, just more slowly
The function immediately returns -1 on the next iteration
It only breaks when the target isn't in the array
items.answer: The loop runs forever, re-checking the same `mid` every time, since the search range never shrinks
items.explanation_md: Binary search's termination depends entirely on `lo`/`hi` moving closer together every iteration. If a branch forgets to update them, `mid` is recomputed identically forever — an infinite loop.

items.prompt_md: Using the inclusive `while lo <= hi` loop, what off-by-one bug would `hi = mid` (instead of `hi = mid - 1`) introduce in the `nums[mid] > target` branch?
options: It can cause an infinite loop, since `mid` itself has already been ruled out but stays inside the next range
It has no effect on correctness or termination
It causes the function to skip over the correct answer entirely
It only matters when the array has an even number of elements
items.answer: It can cause an infinite loop, since `mid` itself has already been ruled out but stays inside the next range
items.explanation_md: Once `nums[mid] > target` rules out index `mid`, leaving it inside `nums[lo..hi]` means `lo == hi == mid` can repeat forever without the range ever shrinking to empty.

items.prompt_md: Which of these is the most common cause of an infinite loop in a hand-written binary search?
options: Forgetting to move `lo` or `hi` past `mid` in at least one branch, so the range never shrinks
Computing `mid` with integer division instead of floating-point division
Returning `mid` instead of `-1` when the loop ends without finding the target
items.answer: Forgetting to move `lo` or `hi` past `mid` in at least one branch, so the range never shrinks
items.explanation_md: As long as every branch strictly advances `lo` past `mid` or pulls `hi` back before `mid`, the range shrinks every iteration. Skipping that update in any branch is what stalls the loop.

items.prompt_md: Roughly how many comparisons does binary search need in the worst case for a sorted array of 1,000,000 elements?
items.explanation_md: log2(1,000,000) is about 20, so binary search needs only around 20 comparisons even though the array has a million elements — the hallmark of O(log n).

items.prompt_md: Why is it safe for the function to return -1 once `lo > hi`?
options: Because the loop invariant guarantees the target would still be somewhere in `nums[lo..hi]` if it existed, so an empty range means it's genuinely absent
Because -1 is a sentinel value that always means the array was empty to begin with
Because `lo > hi` only happens when the array has duplicate values
Because the array must be re-scanned linearly before giving up
items.answer: Because the loop invariant guarantees the target would still be somewhere in `nums[lo..hi]` if it existed, so an empty range means it's genuinely absent
items.explanation_md: Every step preserves "if present, the target is in `nums[lo..hi]`." Once that range is empty, the invariant is only satisfiable if the target was never there at all.

items.prompt_md: You're given a sorted array of integers and need to return the index of a specific target value, or -1 if it isn't present.
items.explanation_md: Sorted data plus "find the position of a value" is the direct trigger for halving the search range with a midpoint comparison instead of scanning every element.

items.prompt_md: You need to determine whether a specific value exists anywhere in a sorted array of a billion elements, and you can only afford a few dozen comparisons.
items.explanation_md: The array is sorted and the comparison budget rules out an O(n) scan — halving the range on each comparison gets an answer in about log2(n) steps.

items.prompt_md: You're given an unsorted array of integers and must find the indices of two numbers that add up to a target sum.
items.explanation_md: The array isn't sorted and sorting it would destroy the original indices you need to return, so there's no boundary to binary search — a hash map of value-to-index finds the complement in one pass instead.

items.prompt_md: Given a sorted array, you need the smallest index `i` such that `nums[i] >= target` (the target's insertion point).
items.explanation_md: "Sorted array, find the boundary where a condition flips" is exactly the binary-search trigger, even though this asks for an insertion point rather than an exact match.

items.prompt_md: You're scanning an unsorted list of integers and need to know, as fast as possible, whether any value appears more than once.
items.explanation_md: There's no sortedness to exploit and no boundary condition — a seen-set catches a repeat the moment it's scanned, which is a hashing problem, not a search-range problem.

items.prompt_md: A sorted array of unique integers is given, and you must decide if a particular number is present, running this same query thousands of times against the same array.
items.explanation_md: Sorted data and a per-query cost budget both point at halving the range each comparison rather than re-scanning linearly every time.

items.prompt_md: You need to count how many elements in a sorted array are strictly less than a given threshold value.
items.explanation_md: Counting elements below a threshold in sorted data reduces to finding the boundary index where the condition flips, then reading off its position — a direct binary-search target.

items.prompt_md: You're given an unsorted array and need to group all the strings in it into sets of anagrams of one another.
items.explanation_md: Grouping by a derived key (like sorted letters or a letter-count signature) is a hashing problem — there's no sorted boundary to search since order among the strings doesn't matter.

items.prompt_md: Given a sorted array, you need the index of the last occurrence of a target value that may appear multiple times in a row.
items.explanation_md: Finding the last occurrence is still a boundary in sorted data — the flip point between "still the target" and "past the target" — which binary search finds in O(log n) instead of scanning past every duplicate.

items.prompt_md: You have a sorted array of distinct integers and need to determine whether the number `x` appears in it, and you only get one query.
items.explanation_md: Even for a single query, sorted data means a midpoint comparison can eliminate half the array immediately, which is strictly less work than checking every element in a linear scan.

items.prompt_md: You need to find how many times a specific number appears in a sorted array, using the fact that all its occurrences sit next to each other.
items.explanation_md: Finding the first and last occurrence are each a boundary-search on sorted data, and subtracting the two indices gives the count in O(log n) total instead of scanning every match.

items.prompt_md: You're given a sorted array of integers and a target, and must decide the exact index where the target sits, knowing the array can be tens of millions of elements long.
items.explanation_md: Large sorted data with an index-lookup goal is the canonical binary-search shape — halving the range keeps the cost logarithmic regardless of how large the array grows.
